title: Environmental Monitoring Excursion in Grade A Fill Suite

preFill.initialObservation: Particle count exceeded the Grade A alert threshold for 12 minutes during routine environmental monitoring on shift A.

gateAnswers.question: What happened and when was it observed?
gateAnswers.answer: On 18 May 2026 at 08:15 (Shift A), continuous environmental monitoring in the Grade A Fill Suite (Vaccine Filling Line 2) recorded ≥0.5 µm particle counts above the Grade A alert limit for 12 minutes during active vaccine filling. The excursion was flagged by the EM system at counter HEPA-FF-L2-07 and confirmed against the SOP-EM-014 trend.

gateAnswers.question: What is the potential quality or GMP impact?
gateAnswers.answer: Potential Major GMP impact. The excursion occurred in a classified Grade A aseptic zone during active vaccine filling, so sterility assurance for the units filled inside the exposure window must be assessed. No sterility failure is confirmed yet, which keeps this Major rather than Critical pending the batch impact review.

gateAnswers.question: What immediate containment is already in place?
gateAnswers.answer: Vaccine Filling Line 2 was placed on immediate engineering hold and batches VAC-26-0518-A and VAC-26-0518-B were quarantined pending QA disposition. Environmental re-test was initiated and HEPA-FF-L2-07 was flagged for integrity inspection before the suite returns to routine operation.

gateAnswers.question: Which product, batch, lot, equipment, or area is affected?
gateAnswers.answer: Grade A Fill Suite, Vaccine Filling Line 2, HEPA filter HEPA-FF-L2-07. Product impact is limited to vaccine batches VAC-26-0518-A and VAC-26-0518-B filled during Shift A. Source records: EM trend report (SOP-EM-014) and the filling-line event log.

gateAnswers.question: What evidence is needed to confirm the root cause?
gateAnswers.answer: Confirm via a HEPA integrity / DOP test on HEPA-FF-L2-07, a review of the SOP-PM-HEPA-001 preventive-maintenance interval and filter age, the EM trend history for repeat alerts, and the SOP-FF-009 line event log to correlate the excursion with filling activity.

gateAnswers.question: How will effectiveness be verified?
gateAnswers.answer: Effectiveness is verified when post-remediation EM monitoring shows no Grade A alert or action excursions across at least three consecutive filling campaigns, the requalified HEPA passes integrity testing, and the revised maintenance interval is reflected in the planner with no recurrence over 90 days.

factors.rationale: The excursion occurred in a critical aseptic processing area.
The exposure was time-bound and can be assessed against batch records.
Potential batch impact requires QA assessment.

impact.rationale: Nova detected a Major deviation because the event occurred in a controlled filling area, involved a vaccine batch, and required immediate containment. The case is suitable for a structured 8D CAPA workflow.

disposisi.rationale: Excursion in a critical aseptic filling area involving active vaccine batches warrants full 8D investigation. Major severity confirmed. Assigned to Fill-Finish for structured corrective action workflow.

title: GMP Documentation Gap in Material Transfer Records

preFill.findingDescription: Material transfer records for three lots were completed after shift handover and lacked documented second-person verification.

gateAnswers.question: What happened and when was it observed?
gateAnswers.answer: During internal audit IA-2026-QA-042 on 14 May 2026, QA Compliance found that warehouse material transfer records for three lots were completed after shift handover and lacked documented second-person verification, contrary to SOP-WH-021 and SOP-DOC-004.

gateAnswers.question: What is the potential quality or GMP impact?
gateAnswers.answer: Major GMP and data-integrity impact. Contemporaneous recording and independent verification underpin record reliability under GMP Chapter 4 and the BPOM 2023 Data Integrity guidance; the gap weakens audit defensibility for the affected transfers even though no material mix-up has been identified.

gateAnswers.question: What immediate containment is already in place?
gateAnswers.answer: The three affected material transfer records were segregated for a QA data-integrity review, and a temporary second-person verification check was reinstated at shift handover while the records are reconciled against physical stock.

gateAnswers.question: Which product, batch, lot, equipment, or area is affected?
gateAnswers.answer: Warehouse material transfer documentation for three lots recorded across the shift-handover window. Governing records: SOP-WH-021 (material transfer) and SOP-DOC-004 (documentation practices); finding logged in Q100+ as AUD-2026-0089.

gateAnswers.question: What evidence is needed to confirm the root cause?
gateAnswers.answer: Confirm by reconciling the three transfer records against physical stock and ERP movements, interviewing the shift-handover personnel to map where verification ownership was lost, and reviewing whether SOP-WH-021 explicitly assigns real-time second-person verification.

gateAnswers.question: How will effectiveness be verified?
gateAnswers.answer: Effective when a follow-up record review over the next two months shows 100% contemporaneous completion with documented second-person verification, and the revised SOP plus retraining are confirmed in place with zero repeat observations at the next internal audit.

factors.rationale: Incomplete verification can weaken record reliability.
The issue appears limited and can be reconciled.
The issue may become an external audit observation if repeated.

impact.rationale: Nova classified this audit finding as Major because it involves GMP documentation control and potential data integrity risk across repeated material transfer records.

novaEntries: SOP-WH-021 did not explicitly assign real-time second-person verification ownership for material transfers.
userEntries: Verification was deferred to an end-of-shift reconciliation step instead of being contemporaneous.

novaEntries: Shift handover transferred record completion without confirming who owned the verification step.
No in-process check flagged records completed after the handover window.

confirmedRootCauses: Second-person verification ownership for material transfer records was not assigned across the shift-handover window, so records were completed retrospectively without independent verification.

verification.result: Two-month follow-up review of material transfer records confirmed 100% contemporaneous completion with documented second-person verification across all sampled transfers.

approvals.notes: Planned corrective and preventive actions endorsed for QA submission.
CAPA Plan reviewed — documentation SOP revision and end-of-shift verification checklist are appropriate. Endorsed.
CAPA Plan approved at division level. Execute the actions, then submit the CAPA Actual phase for approval.

disposisi.rationale: Documentation gaps spanning multiple material transfer records indicate systemic non-compliance risk. Major severity confirmed per internal audit classification criteria. 8D workflow assigned to QA Compliance.

schedule.scope: Documentation practices — second-person verification on cleaning and material-transfer logbooks (Fill-Finish & Warehouse).

preFill.description: Hospital pharmacist observed visible particulate matter in one returned vial before administration. The vial remained sealed and was quarantined by the customer.

gateAnswers.question: What happened and when was it observed?
gateAnswers.answer: On 12 May 2026, RS Hasan Sadikin Bandung reported that a hospital pharmacist observed visible particulate matter in one returned Vaccine Multi-dose Vial (lot VAC-26-0419-C) prior to administration. The vial remained sealed and was quarantined by the customer.

gateAnswers.question: What is the potential quality or GMP impact?
gateAnswers.answer: Major product-quality impact. Visible particulate in a parenteral vaccine is a patient-facing quality defect raised by a hospital customer; until the returned vial and retained samples are assessed, batch-wide impact cannot be excluded, though current scope is bounded to a single reported vial.

gateAnswers.question: What immediate containment is already in place?
gateAnswers.answer: The complaint vial is being recovered under a controlled return and retained samples for lot VAC-26-0419-C have been placed on QA review hold. Remaining distributed stock of the lot is monitored for further complaints pending the visual-inspection assessment.

gateAnswers.question: Which product, batch, lot, equipment, or area is affected?
gateAnswers.answer: Vaccine Multi-dose Vial, lot VAC-26-0419-C (expiry 30 Apr 2027), a single reported vial from RS Hasan Sadikin Bandung. Source records: customer photo, complaint intake form, and the batch visual-inspection reconciliation logged in Bizzmine as CMP-2026-0112.

gateAnswers.question: What evidence is needed to confirm the root cause?
gateAnswers.answer: Confirm by assessing the returned vial and retained samples for particulate type (visual and microscopy), reviewing the lot's visual-inspection reconciliation and reject trend for borderline observations, and tracing whether any in-line inspection variance was escalated.

gateAnswers.question: How will effectiveness be verified?
gateAnswers.answer: Effective when the retained-sample assessment defines the particulate source, the strengthened visual-inspection reconciliation and escalation criteria are implemented, and no recurrence of particulate complaints is seen for the product over the following two production campaigns.

factors.rationale: The issue was detected by a customer before administration.
Particulate matter requires product quality and batch impact assessment.
The initial scope is limited but must be verified.

impact.rationale: Nova classified this complaint as Major because it involves visible particulate matter in a distributed vaccine product and requires batch record review, retained sample assessment, and complaint investigation.

nodes.question: Was particulate present in retained samples?
Review inspection reconciliation for the lot family.
nodes.conclusion: Inspection reconciliation escalation may be insufficient.

nodes.question: Assess distribution and customer handling evidence.
nodes.conclusion: External handling remains possible but less likely.

confirmedRootCauses: Visual inspection rejection trend was not escalated after repeated borderline observations in the same lot family.

verification.result: Returned-vial and retained-sample assessment identified the particulate source; two subsequent production campaigns of the product showed no recurrence of particulate complaints.

approvals.notes: Round 1 — analysis and proposed actions reviewed. Visual-inspection escalation rule is sound. Endorsed for division-level review.
Complaint analysis and proposed corrective/preventive actions reviewed and endorsed.
Round 1 analysis approved. Send the customer response, then proceed to closure once the reply window resolves.

disposisi.rationale: Visible particulate matter in a vaccine vial constitutes a critical external complaint requiring batch traceability and root cause investigation. Major severity confirmed. Full 8D CAPA workflow required.

preFill.initialObservation: Operator recorded the wrong line number (Line 3 instead of Line 2) on the daily cleaning logbook entry; the cleaning itself was performed and verified on the correct line.

gateAnswers.question: What happened and when was it observed?
gateAnswers.answer: On 20 May 2026 at 07:30 (Shift A), the operator completing the Solution Prep Room daily cleaning logbook recorded the wrong line identifier — Line 3 instead of Line 2 — for the morning cleaning record. The transposition was noticed during the same-shift supervisor review.

gateAnswers.question: What is the potential quality or GMP impact?
gateAnswers.answer: No GMP or product impact. The cleaning was performed and independently verified on the correct line (Line 2); only the written line identifier was transposed. The entry was corrected the same shift with a single-line GMP correction (struck through, initialed, dated) per SOP-DOC-002, preserving the original entry.

gateAnswers.question: What immediate containment is already in place?
gateAnswers.answer: The entry was corrected contemporaneously with a single-line correction, initialed and dated by the operator and countersigned by the shift supervisor. No batch was affected and no further containment was required.

gateAnswers.question: Which product, batch, lot, equipment, or area is affected?
gateAnswers.answer: Single handwritten logbook entry — Solution Prep Room daily cleaning record, Shift A, 20 May 2026. No product, batch, lot, or equipment qualification affected. Governing record SOP-DOC-002 (good documentation practice).

factors.rationale: A handwritten identifier transposition with no effect on the cleaning performed.
Contemporaneous self-correction preserves the original entry and audit trail.
No batch, lot, or equipment qualification was affected.

impact.rationale: Minor: an isolated good-documentation-practice slip on a cleaning logbook, corrected contemporaneously, with no product, batch, or data-integrity impact.

intakeReviews.notes: Reviewed as an isolated, contemporaneously corrected documentation entry (single-line correction per SOP-DOC-002) with no product, batch, or data-integrity impact — the cleaning was performed and independently verified on the correct line. This is a documented correction, not a quality event, so a full 8D CAPA is disproportionate to the risk. Recommend closing as a logged correction with no CAPA and trending the logbook for any repeat transcription errors.

Concur with QA Compliance. Root cause is a one-off transcription slip self-corrected within the shift; there is no recurrence trend for this logbook over the past 12 months. Initiating a CAPA would not be proportionate to the risk. Approved to reject CAPA initiation — record retained for periodic logbook trending only.

title: Cold-Chain Temperature Excursion in Vaccine Cold Room CR-FF-03

preFill.initialObservation: Cold room CR-FF-03 logged a 2-8 C excursion (peak 10.4 C) for about 95 minutes overnight; the morning shift found the high-temperature alarm active and unacknowledged.

gateAnswers.question: What happened and when was it observed?
gateAnswers.answer: On 12 Apr 2026 at 02:35, cold room CR-FF-03 (finished-goods cold store) exceeded the 2-8 C storage range, peaking at 10.4 C for about 95 minutes. The high-temperature alarm annunciated locally but was found active and unacknowledged by the morning shift at 07:10, confirmed against the SOP-WH-030 temperature trend.

gateAnswers.question: What is the potential quality or GMP impact?
gateAnswers.answer: Potential Major GMP impact. Two vaccine batches in finished-goods storage were exposed to an out-of-range temperature, so a stability and temperature-impact assessment against the labelled 2-8 C range is required before disposition. No stability failure is confirmed yet, which keeps this Major rather than Critical pending the assessment.

gateAnswers.question: What immediate containment is already in place?
gateAnswers.answer: The affected batches VAC-26-0402-A and VAC-26-0402-B were quarantined on QA hold, product was transferred to a qualified backup cold room, and CR-FF-03 was placed on engineering hold pending compressor and alarm inspection.

gateAnswers.question: Which product, batch, lot, equipment, or area is affected?
gateAnswers.answer: Finished-goods cold room CR-FF-03 and the two vaccine batches stored inside during the excursion window (VAC-26-0402-A, VAC-26-0402-B). Source records: cold-room temperature trend (SOP-WH-030) and the alarm event log.

gateAnswers.question: What evidence is needed to confirm the root cause?
gateAnswers.answer: Confirm via the compressor fault log, the alarm-system escalation configuration for CR-FF-03, the on-call response record for the overnight window, and a review of whether the SOP-WH-030 preventive-maintenance check covers after-hours alarm escalation.

gateAnswers.question: How will effectiveness be verified?
gateAnswers.answer: Effective when the reconfigured after-hours escalation passes a documented test alarm, the revised maintenance checklist is in place, and no unacknowledged cold-room excursion recurs across a 90-day monitoring window for all GMP cold rooms.

factors.rationale: An out-of-range excursion in qualified cold storage affects product disposition.
The magnitude and duration must be assessed against the labelled storage range.
Potential batch impact requires a stability and QA assessment.

impact.rationale: Nova detected a Major deviation because two vaccine batches in qualified cold storage were exposed to an out-of-range temperature requiring stability assessment and containment.

fiveWhys.question: Why did cold room CR-FF-03 exceed 8 C overnight?
fiveWhys.novaSuggestion: The compressor cut out and the temperature rose without intervention.
fiveWhys.userAnswer: The lead compressor faulted overnight and the temperature drifted up without any corrective intervention.

fiveWhys.question: Why was the rising temperature not addressed for 95 minutes?
fiveWhys.novaSuggestion: The high-temperature alarm was not acknowledged because no one was alerted after hours.
fiveWhys.userAnswer: The alarm annunciated locally in an unstaffed area after hours, so no one was alerted to respond.

fiveWhys.question: Why was no one alerted to the after-hours alarm?
fiveWhys.novaSuggestion: The alarm annunciated locally but did not escalate to the on-call engineer.
fiveWhys.userAnswer: The alarm did not escalate beyond the local panel to the on-call engineer's pager.

fiveWhys.question: Why did the alarm not escalate to on-call?
fiveWhys.novaSuggestion: The escalation path for CR-FF-03 was never configured after a controller upgrade.
fiveWhys.userAnswer: The escalation route for CR-FF-03 was not reconfigured in the alarm system after the 2025 controller upgrade.

fiveWhys.question: Why was the missing escalation not detected before the excursion?
fiveWhys.novaSuggestion: After-hours escalation was not part of the preventive-maintenance test checklist for cold rooms.
fiveWhys.userAnswer: After-hours alarm-escalation testing was not included in the preventive-maintenance checklist for GMP cold rooms, so the gap went undetected.

confirmedRootCauses: The cold-room compressor's high-temperature alarm was not configured to escalate to the on-call engineer after hours, so a 2-8 C excursion ran unaddressed until the morning shift.

verification.result: After-hours alarm escalation was reconfigured and passed a documented test alarm; a 90-day monitoring window across all GMP cold rooms showed no recurrence of unacknowledged excursions.

approvals.notes: Containment, corrective and preventive actions for the cold-chain excursion are appropriate. Endorsed for QA sign-off.
Stability assessment, alarm-escalation fix and SOP revision reviewed and endorsed.
SME co-approval (Major): the stability-impact rationale and the escalation remediation adequately address sterility-unrelated cold-chain risk. Concur.
CAPA approved and closed as Audit Ready. Effectiveness verification complete.

disposisi.rationale: Out-of-range excursion affecting two vaccine batches in qualified cold storage warrants a full 8D investigation. Major severity confirmed; assigned to Fill-Finish for structured corrective action.

title: Overdue Calibration on QC Balance Not Flagged

preFill.findingDescription: A QC analytical balance was found in service three weeks past its calibration due date; the overdue status was not flagged by the manual tracking spreadsheet.

gateAnswers.question: What happened and when was it observed?
gateAnswers.answer: During internal audit IA-2026-QA-031 on 17 Mar 2026, QA Compliance found a QC analytical balance in active use three weeks past its calibration due date. The overdue status had not been flagged by the manual calibration-tracking spreadsheet, contrary to SOP-QC-012 and SOP-CAL-002.

gateAnswers.question: What is the potential quality or GMP impact?
gateAnswers.answer: Major GMP impact. A balance used for QC release testing operating past its calibration due date places the reliability of results generated in that window in question under GMP Chapter 3 and Annex 15, requiring a retrospective data review even though no out-of-trend result has yet been identified.

gateAnswers.question: What immediate containment is already in place?
gateAnswers.answer: The balance was removed from service and recalibrated, and QC results generated since the last valid calibration were placed under a data-integrity review while all QC balance statuses were reconciled against the master schedule.

gateAnswers.question: Which product, batch, lot, equipment, or area is affected?
gateAnswers.answer: One QC analytical balance and the QC results it generated between its calibration due date and removal from service. Governing records: SOP-QC-012 (calibration tracking) and SOP-CAL-002; finding logged in Q100+ as AUD-2026-0156.

gateAnswers.question: What evidence is needed to confirm the root cause?
gateAnswers.answer: Confirm by comparing the manual tracking spreadsheet against the master calibration schedule, reviewing the post-calibration verification result to bound any data impact, and assessing whether the tracking method has an automated overdue flag.

gateAnswers.question: How will effectiveness be verified?
gateAnswers.answer: Effective when calibration tracking is migrated to the centralised register with automated due/overdue flags, monthly reconciliation is evidenced, and no overdue-calibration instrument is found in service at the next internal audit.

factors.rationale: A balance used for release testing directly affects result reliability.
Operating past the due date requires a retrospective data review.

factors.value: Results since last calibration under review
factors.rationale: The window of affected results must be bounded and assessed.

impact.rationale: Nova classified this audit finding as Major because an overdue-calibration QC instrument affects the reliability of release-test data and indicates a tracking-control gap.

novaEntries: Calibration due dates were tracked in a manual spreadsheet with no automated overdue flag.
userEntries: The spreadsheet was not reconciled against the master calibration schedule on a defined cadence.

novaEntries: Ownership for updating the spreadsheet was unclear after a QC scheduling change.
No periodic cross-check compared in-service instruments against their calibration status.

confirmedRootCauses: Calibration due-date tracking for QC balances relied on a manual spreadsheet that was not reconciled against the master calibration schedule, so an overdue balance stayed in service without a flag.

verification.result: Calibration tracking was migrated to the centralised register with automated overdue flags; two monthly reconciliations were evidenced and no overdue-calibration instrument was found in service on follow-up review.

approvals.notes: CAPA Plan - recalibration, data review and register migration endorsed for QA submission.
CAPA Plan reviewed - the register migration and reconciliation cadence address the tracking gap. Endorsed.
CAPA Plan approved at division level. Execute the actions, then submit the CAPA Actual phase.
CAPA Actual - executed recalibration, completed data review and register migration confirmed.
CAPA Actual reviewed - executed actions and evidence are complete. Endorsed for closure.
CAPA Actual approved. Proceed to QA final verification and closure.
Closing-completeness check passed and effectiveness verified. CAPA closed as Audit Ready.

disposisi.rationale: An overdue-calibration QC release instrument indicates a systemic tracking-control gap with potential data impact. Major severity confirmed; 8D workflow assigned to QA Compliance.

schedule.scope: Equipment calibration control - QC instruments calibration tracking and status (Quality Control).

preFill.description: Distributor warehouse scanners could not read the secondary-carton barcode on multiple units from the lot during goods-in; manual entry was required for receipt.

gateAnswers.question: What happened and when was it observed?
gateAnswers.answer: On 14 Feb 2026, PT Kimia Farma Trading & Distribution reported that warehouse scanners could not read the secondary-carton barcode on multiple units of lot VAC-26-0118-B during goods-in, forcing manual data entry to complete receipt.

gateAnswers.question: What is the potential quality or GMP impact?
gateAnswers.answer: Major distribution and traceability impact. The secondary-carton barcode is the scan key for lot tracking through the supply chain; an unscannable print degrades traceability and goods-in control, though the vial, label, seal and product remain conforming.

gateAnswers.question: What immediate containment is already in place?
gateAnswers.answer: Remaining distributed stock of lot VAC-26-0118-B was placed on a scan-grade re-inspection hold, and the distributor was provided an interim manual-receipt instruction while affected cartons are reworked and re-verified.

gateAnswers.question: Which product, batch, lot, equipment, or area is affected?
gateAnswers.answer: Vaccine Multi-dose Vial, lot VAC-26-0118-B (expiry 31 Jan 2027) secondary cartons distributed to PT Kimia Farma. Source records: distributor scanner report, complaint intake form, and packaging line-setup records logged in Bizzmine as CMP-2026-0073.

gateAnswers.question: What evidence is needed to confirm the root cause?
gateAnswers.answer: Confirm by measuring the ANSI scan-grade / print contrast on retained cartons of the lot, reviewing the packaging line-setup record for a barcode verification step, and checking whether in-process scan checks were performed during the run.

gateAnswers.question: How will effectiveness be verified?
gateAnswers.answer: Effective when an ANSI scan-grade verification is added at line setup and in-process, the distributor confirms successful scanning of reworked stock, and no barcode-readability complaint recurs over the following two packaging campaigns.

factors.rationale: An unscannable barcode disrupts distributor receipt and handling.

factors.value: Barcode is the lot scan key
factors.rationale: Readability underpins supply-chain track-and-trace.
Initial scope is bounded to one lot and can be reworked.

impact.rationale: Nova classified this complaint as Major because an unscannable secondary-carton barcode degrades supply-chain traceability and goods-in control for a distributed vaccine lot.

fiveWhys.question: Why could the distributor not scan the secondary-carton barcode?
fiveWhys.novaSuggestion: The barcode print contrast was below the scan-grade threshold.
fiveWhys.userAnswer: The printed barcode had low contrast / print-quality grade below the readable scan-grade threshold.

fiveWhys.question: Why was a low-contrast barcode produced on the run?
fiveWhys.novaSuggestion: The carton printer settings drifted and were not caught at setup.
fiveWhys.userAnswer: The carton coder ribbon/contrast had drifted and the print quality was not verified at line setup.

fiveWhys.question: Why was the print quality not verified at setup?
fiveWhys.novaSuggestion: Line setup had no scan-grade barcode verification step.
fiveWhys.userAnswer: The packaging line-setup checklist had no ANSI scan-grade barcode verification, only a visual presence check.

fiveWhys.question: Why was scan-grade verification not part of the setup control?
fiveWhys.novaSuggestion: Barcode acceptance criteria were not defined as a verifiable in-process check.
fiveWhys.userAnswer: The SOP defined a readable-barcode requirement but not a measurable scan-grade acceptance check at setup and in-process.

confirmedRootCauses: Secondary-carton barcode print contrast was not verified against the scan-grade acceptance criteria at line setup, so a low-contrast print batch passed to distribution.

verification.result: An ANSI scan-grade verification was added at setup and in-process; the distributor confirmed successful scanning of reworked stock and no readability complaint recurred over the following two packaging campaigns.

approvals.notes: Round 1 - analysis and proposed actions reviewed. Scan-grade verification at setup is the right control. Endorsed for division-level review.
Complaint analysis and proposed corrective/preventive actions reviewed and endorsed.
Round 1 analysis approved. Send the customer response, then proceed to closure once the reply window resolves.
Round 2 - closure reviewed. Rework verified and the in-process control is in place. Endorsed.
Closure evidence and effectiveness verification complete. Endorsed for final closure.
Round 2 closure approved. CAPA closed as Audit Ready.

disposisi.rationale: An unscannable secondary-carton barcode on a distributed vaccine lot affects traceability and distributor receipt. Major severity confirmed; full 8D complaint workflow required.

title: Out-of-Specification Fill Volume at In-Process Check - Line 1

preFill.initialObservation: A routine in-process fill-volume check on Line 1 returned three consecutive vials below the lower fill-volume limit; the line was paused for investigation.

gateAnswers.question: What happened and when was it observed?
gateAnswers.answer: On 5 Jun 2026 at 13:10, a routine in-process fill-volume check on Vaccine Filling Line 1 returned three consecutive vials below the lower fill-volume limit. The operator paused the line and flagged batch VAC-26-0605-A for investigation.

gateAnswers.question: What is the potential quality or GMP impact?
gateAnswers.answer: Potential under-fill affecting dose delivery for the units in the check window. Batch impact is being assessed; classification pending QA review.
gateAnswers.improvementHint: Quantify the deviation magnitude (how far below the limit) and bound the number of vials potentially affected to strengthen the impact statement.

gateAnswers.question: What immediate containment is already in place?
gateAnswers.answer: Line 1 was paused and batch VAC-26-0605-A placed on hold pending investigation.

gateAnswers.question: Which product, batch, lot, equipment, or area is affected?
gateAnswers.answer: Vaccine Filling Line 1, filler FILLER-FF-L1-03, batch VAC-26-0605-A. Source record: in-process fill-volume check log (SOP-IPC-003).

gateAnswers.question: What evidence is needed to confirm the root cause?
gateAnswers.answer: Pending - the filler dosing setup and recent changeover records still need review.
gateAnswers.improvementHint: List the specific evidence to gather (pump calibration, changeover record, fill-weight trend) so the investigation has a clear confirmation path.

factors.rationale: Under-fill can affect delivered dose and must be assessed.
The event occurred during active aseptic filling.
Initial scope is bounded to one batch pending review.

impact.rationale: Nova flagged a potential Major deviation: an in-process under-fill on an active filling line affecting a vaccine batch. Final classification pending QA intake review.

title: Suspected Loss of Sterility Assurance - Aseptic Intervention Breach

preFill.initialObservation: During a manual intervention on Line 2, an operator's gauntlet contacted a non-sterile surface inside the Grade A zone; the breach was observed and the batch flagged for sterility-assurance review.

gateAnswers.question: What happened and when was it observed?
gateAnswers.answer: On 4 Jun 2026 at 15:05, during a manual intervention on Vaccine Filling Line 2, an operator's gauntlet contacted a non-sterile surface inside the Grade A zone. The breach was observed in real time by the line monitor and the intervention was logged.

gateAnswers.question: What is the potential quality or GMP impact?
gateAnswers.answer: Critical GMP impact. A loss of aseptic conditions in a Grade A filling zone places sterility assurance for the units filled around the intervention window in question. Sterility is a patient-safety-critical attribute for a parenteral vaccine and cannot be assured by testing alone, so the affected units require full sterility-assurance assessment.

gateAnswers.question: What immediate containment is already in place?
gateAnswers.answer: Filling on Line 2 was stopped, batches VAC-26-0604-A and VAC-26-0604-B were quarantined on QA hold, and the affected zone was flagged for environmental monitoring and line re-clearance before any further filling.

gateAnswers.question: Which product, batch, lot, equipment, or area is affected?
gateAnswers.answer: Grade A Fill Suite, Vaccine Filling Line 2 (isolator ISO-FF-L2-01) and the two vaccine batches filled around the intervention window (VAC-26-0604-A, VAC-26-0604-B). Source records: aseptic intervention log (SOP-ASEPTIC-002) and the EM record for the session.

factors.rationale: Loss of aseptic conditions directly challenges sterility assurance.
Sterility is a patient-safety-critical attribute and cannot be assured by testing alone.
Both batches in the intervention window require assessment.

impact.rationale: Nova classified this deviation as Critical because an aseptic breach in a Grade A filling zone challenges sterility assurance for a parenteral vaccine, requiring SME (microbiology) consultation and full 8D investigation.

fiveWhys.question: Why is sterility assurance of the affected units in question?
fiveWhys.novaSuggestion: An operator gauntlet contacted a non-sterile surface during a manual intervention.
fiveWhys.userAnswer: An operator's gauntlet contacted a non-sterile surface during a manual intervention on Line 2.

fiveWhys.question: Why did the gauntlet contact a non-sterile surface during the intervention?
fiveWhys.novaSuggestion: The intervention required reaching across the Grade A zone without a defined no-touch path.

fiveWhys.question: Why was a validated no-touch reach path not available for this intervention?
fiveWhys.novaSuggestion: The aseptic intervention procedure may not map a validated reach path for this stoppage type.

disposisi.rationale: An aseptic breach in a Grade A filling zone places sterility assurance of two vaccine batches in question. Critical severity confirmed; full 8D with SME (microbiology) consultation required.

title: BPOM Inspection Finding - EM Data Trending Not Documented per Schedule

preFill.findingDescription: Periodic environmental-monitoring trend review for the Grade A/B filling areas was not documented at the frequency defined in the EM program during Q1 2026.

gateAnswers.question: What happened and when was it observed?
gateAnswers.answer: During the BPOM inspection on 28 May 2026 (EXT-2026-BPOM-007), the inspector found that the periodic environmental-monitoring trend review for the Grade A/B filling areas was not documented at the frequency defined in the EM program for the Q1 2026 period.

gateAnswers.question: What is the potential quality or GMP impact?
gateAnswers.answer: Major regulatory and GMP impact. EM trend review is a required element of contamination-control oversight under Annex 1; missing the documented review frequency weakens the ability to detect adverse trends early, even though individual EM results in the period were within limits.

gateAnswers.question: What immediate containment is already in place?
gateAnswers.answer: A retrospective EM trend review for Q1 2026 was completed and confirmed no adverse trend, and a named owner with a calendar trigger was temporarily assigned to the periodic review pending the SOP fix.

gateAnswers.question: Which product, batch, lot, equipment, or area is affected?
gateAnswers.answer: EM trend-review documentation for the Grade A/B filling areas across the Q1 2026 period. Governing records: SOP-EM-014 and SOP-EM-TREND-002; finding logged in Q100+ as AUD-2026-0301 against BPOM inspection EXT-2026-BPOM-007.

gateAnswers.question: What evidence is needed to confirm the root cause?
gateAnswers.answer: Confirm by reviewing the EM trend-review SOP for an assigned owner and frequency trigger, and by mapping the Q1 staffing change to identify where review ownership was lost.
gateAnswers.improvementHint: Attach the SOP ownership extract and the staffing-change handover record as evidence to close the confirmation loop.

factors.rationale: An external regulatory finding requires a documented, time-bound response.
Missing the defined review frequency weakens contamination-control oversight.
Scope is bounded to the Q1 review period for the filling areas.

impact.rationale: Nova classified this external audit finding as Major because a missed EM trend-review frequency is an Annex 1 contamination-control oversight gap raised by BPOM and requires a time-bound regulatory response.

novaEntries: The EM trend-review SOP defined the frequency but did not assign a named owner or a calendar trigger for the periodic review.
Trend-review responsibility shifted during a Q1 staffing change without a documented handover.

disposisi.rationale: An external BPOM finding on EM trend-review frequency requires a time-bound CAPA with a documented report-back to the regulator. Major severity confirmed; 8D workflow assigned to QA Compliance.

schedule.scope: Environmental-monitoring program - trend review frequency and documentation for Grade A/B filling areas.

title: Cosmetic Carton Print Smudge - Complaint Intake Review

preFill.description: Pharmacy reported a faint ink smudge on the outer carton print of one unit. The vial, label, seal, and product were unaffected and within specification.

gateAnswers.question: What happened and when was it observed?
gateAnswers.answer: On 8 Apr 2026, Apotek Sehat Sentosa reported a faint ink smudge on the outer carton print of a single Vaccine Multi-dose Vial unit (lot VAC-26-0312-D). The vial, primary label, seal, and product were unaffected.

gateAnswers.question: What is the potential quality or GMP impact?
gateAnswers.answer: No product-quality or GMP impact. The observation is limited to a cosmetic mark on the outer carton; the vial, label, seal, and product conform to specification and remain fully usable.

gateAnswers.question: Which product, batch, lot, equipment, or area is affected?
gateAnswers.answer: A single reported outer carton from lot VAC-26-0312-D. No primary packaging, label content, seal, or product attribute is affected. Source record: customer photo and complaint intake form CMP-2026-0061.

factors.rationale: A cosmetic mark on secondary packaging with no functional effect.
No primary packaging, label content, or product attribute affected.
Isolated to one reported unit with no recurrence signal.

impact.rationale: Minor: an isolated cosmetic outer-carton print observation with no product, label, seal, or data-integrity impact.

intakeReviews.notes: Cosmetic outer-carton print smudge with no impact to the vial, label, seal, or product quality; the unit conforms to specification. Logged and acknowledged to the customer as a cosmetic observation. A full complaint CAPA is disproportionate to the risk - recommend closing without CAPA and trending carton-print appearance for any recurrence.

Concur with QA. Isolated cosmetic print observation with no product or safety impact and no recurrence trend for the lot family. Approved to reject CAPA initiation; retain for periodic carton-print appearance trending only.
